docs.title: Build, analyze, and automate hydraulic simulations with Python
What is pyKorf?
docs.text: pyKorf is a comprehensive Python library that enables programmatic manipulation of KORF hydraulic simulation models. KORF is widely used in the oil & gas, chemical, and process industries for pipeline hydraulic calculations.

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text: Feature Description Load & Save Read and write .kdf files with full fidelity Edit Models Modify element parameters, add/remove elements, update connectivity Multi-Case Handle multiple simulation scenarios in a single file Validate Check model integrity and KDF format compliance Export Export to JSON, YAML, Excel, and CSV formats Visualize Generate interactive network diagrams with PyVis Automate Control the KORF GUI programmatically (Windows) Type Safe Full Pydantic models for data validation

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
# Basic installation
pip install pykorf

# With all features
pip install pykorf[all]

# Development installation
git clone https://github.com/pykorf/pykorf.git
cd pykorf
pip install -e ".[dev]"

docs.title: Quick Example
docs.text:
from pykorf import Model, Query, attr

# Load a model
model = Model("Pumpcases.kdf")

# Inspect the model
print(f"Version: {model.version}")
print(f"Cases: {model.general.case_descriptions}")
print(f"Pipes: {model.num_pipes}")

# Modify a pipe
model.update_element("L1", {"LEN": 200, "TFLOW": "80;90;60"})

# Query elements
large_pipes = Query(model).pipes.where(attr("diameter_inch") == "8").all()

# Save changes
model.save("Pumpcases_modified.kdf")

def get_param(self, param: str) -> Optional["KdfRecord"]:
    """Return the record for a given parameter, or *None* if missing.

    Example:
        >>> from pykorf.definitions import Feed
        >>> rec = model.feeds[1].get_param(Feed.NAME)
        >>> rec.update(["EXP DRUM", "FEED"])
    """
    return self._get(param)

docs.title: get_fluid
docs.text:
get_fluid() -> 'Fluid'

Extract fluid properties from this pipe.

Returns:

Name Type Description A 'Fluid'

class:Fluid instance populated with the pipe's

'Fluid'

current fluid properties.

Example::

fluid = pipe.get_fluid()
print(f"Temperature: {fluid.temp_inlet[0]}°C")
print(f"Density: {fluid.liquid_density[0]}kg/m³")
Source code in pykorf\elements\pipe.py 
def get_fluid(self) -> "Fluid":
    """Extract fluid properties from this pipe.

    Returns:
        A :class:`Fluid` instance populated with the pipe's
        current fluid properties.

    Example::

        fluid = pipe.get_fluid()
        print(f"Temperature: {fluid.temp_inlet[0]}°C")
        print(f"Density: {fluid.liquid_density[0]}kg/m³")
    """
    from pykorf.fluid import Fluid

    # Helper to extract values
    def get_values(param: str, default: list = None) -> list:
        vals = self._values(param)
        if not vals:
            return default or []
        # Remove unit if present (last element is string)
        if vals and isinstance(vals[-1], str):
            return [float(v) for v in vals[:-1] if _is_num(v)]
        return [float(v) for v in vals if _is_num(v)]

    def get_scalar(param: str, default: float = 0.0) -> float:
        vals = get_values(param)
        return vals[0] if vals else default

    # Extract operating conditions
    temp_vals = get_values(PipeParams.TEMP, [25.0, 25.0, 25.0])
    pres_vals = get_values(PipeParams.PRES, [100.0, 100.0, 100.0])
    lf_vals = get_values(PipeParams.LF, [1.0, 1.0, 1.0])

    # Handle multi-case values
    num_cases = len(temp_vals) // 3

    if num_cases > 1:
        # Multi-case: extract per-case values
        temp_inlet = [temp_vals[i * 3] for i in range(num_cases)]
        temp_outlet = [temp_vals[i * 3 + 1] for i in range(num_cases)]
        temp_average = [temp_vals[i * 3 + 2] for i in range(num_cases)]
        pres_inlet = [pres_vals[i * 3] for i in range(num_cases)]
        pres_outlet = [pres_vals[i * 3 + 1] for i in range(num_cases)]
        pres_average = [pres_vals[i * 3 + 2] for i in range(num_cases)]
        lf = [lf_vals[i * 3] for i in range(num_cases)]
    else:
        temp_inlet = [temp_vals[0]] if temp_vals else [25.0]
        temp_outlet = [temp_vals[1]] if len(temp_vals) > 1 else [25.0]
        temp_average = [temp_vals[2]] if len(temp_vals) > 2 else [25.0]
        pres_inlet = [pres_vals[0]] if pres_vals else [100.0]
        pres_outlet = [pres_vals[1]] if len(pres_vals) > 1 else [100.0]
        pres_average = [pres_vals[2]] if len(pres_vals) > 2 else [100.0]
        lf = [lf_vals[0]] if lf_vals else [1.0]

    return Fluid(
        temp_inlet=temp_inlet,
        temp_outlet=temp_outlet,
        temp_average=temp_average,
        pres_inlet=pres_inlet,
        pres_outlet=pres_outlet,
        pres_average=pres_average,
        liquid_fraction=lf,
        liquid_density=get_values(PipeParams.LIQDEN, [1000.0]),
        liquid_viscosity=get_values(PipeParams.LIQVISC, [1.0]),
        liquid_surface_tension=get_values(PipeParams.LIQSUR, [62.4]),
        liquid_conductivity=get_values(PipeParams.LIQCON, [0.5]),
        liquid_cp=get_values(PipeParams.LIQCP, [1.0]),
        liquid_mw=get_values(PipeParams.LIQMW, [18.0]),
        vapor_density=get_values(PipeParams.VAPDEN, [0.0]),
        vapor_viscosity=get_values(PipeParams.VAPVISC, [0.0]),
        vapor_conductivity=get_values(PipeParams.VAPCON, [0.025]),
        vapor_cp=get_values(PipeParams.VAPCP, [1.0]),
        vapor_mw=get_values(PipeParams.VAPMW, [0.0]),
        vapor_z=get_values(PipeParams.VAPZ, [0.0]),
        vapor_k=get_values(PipeParams.VAPK, [0.0]),
    )

docs.title: set_flow
docs.text:
set_flow(flow: list[str | float | int] | str) -> None

Set the pipe total flow for one or more cases.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
flow: Either a semicolon-delimited string "50;55;20" or a list [50, 55, 20]. A single scalar sets all cases to the same value.

Example::

pipe.set_flow("60;65;25")
pipe.set_flow([60, 65, 25])
pipe.set_flow(70)  # all cases → 70
Source code in pykorf\elements\pipe.py 
def set_flow(self, flow: list[str | float | int] | str) -> None:
    """Set the pipe total flow for one or more cases.

    Parameters
    ----------
    flow:
        Either a semicolon-delimited string ``"50;55;20"`` or a list
        ``[50, 55, 20]``.  A single scalar sets all cases to the
        same value.

    Example::

        pipe.set_flow("60;65;25")
        pipe.set_flow([60, 65, 25])
        pipe.set_flow(70)  # all cases → 70
    """
    if isinstance(flow, (list, tuple)):
        flow_str = join_cases(flow)
    elif isinstance(flow, (int, float)):
        flow_str = str(flow)
    else:
        flow_str = str(flow)

    rec = self._get(PipeParams.TFLOW)
    if rec is None:
        return
    # values layout: [input_string, calc_numeric, unit]
    new_vals = [flow_str] + rec.values[1:]
    self._set(PipeParams.TFLOW, new_vals)

docs.title: set_fluid
docs.text:
set_fluid(fluid: 'Fluid') -> None

Apply fluid properties to this pipe.

This method updates all fluid-related parameters in the KDF file directly, eliminating the need for intermediate text file imports.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default fluid 'Fluid'

A :class:Fluid instance containing all fluid properties.

required

Example::

from pykorf.fluid import Fluid

# Create fluid properties
fluid = Fluid.single_phase_liquid(
    temp=52.25,
    pres=398.7,
    density=570.24,
    viscosity=0.153,
)

# Apply to pipe (directly updates KDF)
pipe.set_fluid(fluid)
Note

This updates the in-memory model. Call model.save() to persist changes to disk.

Source code in pykorf\elements\pipe.py 
def set_fluid(self, fluid: "Fluid") -> None:
    """Apply fluid properties to this pipe.

    This method updates all fluid-related parameters in the KDF file
    directly, eliminating the need for intermediate text file imports.

    Args:
        fluid: A :class:`Fluid` instance containing all fluid properties.

    Example::

        from pykorf.fluid import Fluid

        # Create fluid properties
        fluid = Fluid.single_phase_liquid(
            temp=52.25,
            pres=398.7,
            density=570.24,
            viscosity=0.153,
        )

        # Apply to pipe (directly updates KDF)
        pipe.set_fluid(fluid)

    Note:
        This updates the in-memory model. Call ``model.save()`` to
        persist changes to disk.
    """
    from pykorf.definitions import Pipe as PipeParams

    records = fluid.to_kdf_records()

    # Operating conditions
    self._set(PipeParams.TEMP, records["TEMP"])
    self._set(PipeParams.PRES, records["PRES"])
    self._set(PipeParams.LF, records["LF"])
    self._set(PipeParams.OUTIN, records["OUTIN"])

    # Liquid properties
    self._set(PipeParams.LIQDEN, records["LIQDEN"])
    self._set(PipeParams.LIQVISC, records["LIQVISC"])
    self._set(PipeParams.LIQSUR, records["LIQSUR"])
    self._set(PipeParams.LIQCON, records["LIQCON"])
    self._set(PipeParams.LIQCP, records["LIQCP"])
    self._set(PipeParams.LIQMW, records["LIQMW"])

    # Vapor properties
    self._set(PipeParams.VAPDEN, records["VAPDEN"])
    self._set(PipeParams.VAPVISC, records["VAPVISC"])
    self._set(PipeParams.VAPCON, records["VAPCON"])
    self._set(PipeParams.VAPCP, records["VAPCP"])
    self._set(PipeParams.VAPMW, records["VAPMW"])
    self._set(PipeParams.VAPZ, records["VAPZ"])
    self._set(PipeParams.VAPK, records["VAPK"])

def summary(self) -> dict:
    """Return a dict of key pipe properties (useful for display)."""
    return {
        "name": self.name,
        "diameter_inch": self.diameter_inch,
        "schedule": self.schedule,
        "length_m": self.length_m,
        "material": self.material,
        "flow": self.get_flow(),
        "velocity_m_s": self.velocity,
        "dP_kPa_100m": self.pressure_drop_per_100m,
        "Re": self.reynolds_number,
    }

    q: list,

q: Flow points (list of floats or strings). h: Head points (matching length to q). eff: Efficiency points (fraction, matching length to q). npsh: NPSH-required points (optional, matching length to q). q_unit: Unit for flow (default 'm3/h'). h_unit: Unit for head (default 'm').

Source code in pykorf\elements\pump.py 
def set_curve(
    self,
    q: list,
    h: list,
    eff: list,
    npsh: list | None = None,
    q_unit: str = "m3/h",
    h_unit: str = "m",
) -> None:
    """Set the pump performance curve.

    Parameters
    ----------
    q:        Flow points (list of floats or strings).
    h:        Head points (matching length to *q*).
    eff:      Efficiency points (fraction, matching length to *q*).
    npsh:     NPSH-required points (optional, matching length to *q*).
    q_unit:   Unit for flow (default ``'m3/h'``).
    h_unit:   Unit for head (default ``'m'``).
    """
    self._set("CURQ", [str(v) for v in q] + [q_unit])
    self._set("CURH", [str(v) for v in h] + [h_unit])
    self._set("CUREFF", [str(v) for v in eff] + ["fraction"])
    if npsh is not None:
        self._set("CURNPSH", [str(v) for v in npsh] + ["m"])
    self._set("CURNP", [len(q)])

def set_dp(self, value: str | float) -> None:
    """Override the pump differential pressure.

    Pass an empty string ``""`` to let KORF calculate via the curve.
    """
    rec = self._get("DP")
    if rec:
        new_vals = [str(value)] + rec.values[1:]
        self._set("DP", new_vals)

def set_efficiency(self, value: float) -> None:

Update parameters of the named element.

Parameters:

def update_element(self, name: str, params: dict[str, Any]) -> None:
    """Update parameters of the named element.

    Args:
        name: Element NAME tag (e.g. ``'L1'``, ``'P1'``).
        params: Dict of ``{KDF_PARAM: value}``. Values are set as the first
            token of the record's value list. Special key ``'X'`` and
            ``'Y'`` update the XY record.

    Example:
        ```python
        model.update_element("L1", {"LEN": 200, "TFLOW": "80;90;60"})
        ```
    """
    elem = self.get_element(name)
    rebuild_collections = False
    if Common.NAME in {key.upper() for key in params}:
        name_key = next(k for k in params if k.upper() == Common.NAME)
        new_name = str(params[name_key])
        new_name = self._ensure_unique_name(new_name, current_name=elem.name)
        params[name_key] = new_name  # Update params with unique name
        rebuild_collections = True
    xy_update: dict[str, float] = {}
    for param, value in params.items():
        key = param.upper()
        if key in (Common.X, Common.Y):
            xy_update[key] = float(value)
            continue
        rec = elem._get(key)
        if rec is not None:
            rec.values[0] = str(value)
            rec.raw_line = ""
        else:
            # Try to set via parser (covers params not yet fetched)
            self._parser.set_value(elem.etype, elem.index, key, [str(value)])

    if xy_update:
        self._update_xy(elem, xy_update)

    # Rebuild collections to update _name_map if NAME was changed
    if rebuild_collections:
        self._build_collections()

def add_element(
    self,
    etype: str,
    name: str,
    params: dict[str, Any] | None = None,
) -> BaseElement:
    """Add a new element instance cloned from the template (index 0).

    Parameters
    ----------
    etype:
        KDF element type keyword (e.g. ``'PIPE'``, ``'PUMP'``).
    name:
        Name for the new element (e.g. ``'L10'``, ``'P2'``).
    params:
        Optional parameter overrides applied after creation.

    Returns:
    -------
    The newly created element.
    """
    et = etype.upper()
    if et == Element.PIPE:
        raise ValueError(
            "PIPE cannot be created with add_element(); use "
            "connect_elements(..., pipe_name=...) so the pipe is created "
            "with connectivity."
        )
    return self._add_element_internal(et, name, params)

def add_elements(
    self,
    specs: list[tuple[str, str, dict[str, Any] | None]],
) -> list[BaseElement]:
    """Add multiple elements at once.

    Parameters
    ----------
    specs:
        List of ``(etype, name, params)`` tuples.  ``params`` may be
        *None* for default values.

    Returns:
    -------
    List of newly created elements.
    """
    results = []
    for spec in specs:
        etype, name = spec[0], spec[1]
        params = spec[2] if len(spec) > 2 else None
        results.append(self.add_element(etype, name, params))
    return results

def delete_element(self, name: str) -> None:
    """Delete the named element from the model.

    Removes all records and reindexes remaining elements to be continuous.
    """
    elem = self.get_element(name)
    et = elem.etype
    idx = elem.index

    # If deleting a PIPE, first null out all references to it in equipment
    if et == Element.PIPE:
        from pykorf.connectivity import update_pipe_references

        update_pipe_references(self, idx, 0)

    self._parser.delete_records(et, idx)
    self.compact_indices(et)

Copy an existing element to a new element with a different name.

Connectivity (CON, NOZI, NOZO, NOZL) is cleared on the copy.

docs.title: Parameters

def copy_element(self, src_name: str, dst_name: str) -> BaseElement:
    """Copy an existing element to a new element with a different name.

    Connectivity (CON, NOZI, NOZO, NOZL) is cleared on the copy.

    Parameters
    ----------
    src_name:
        Name of the element to copy.
    dst_name:
        Name for the new copy.

    Returns:
    -------
    The newly created copy.
    """
    dst_name = self._ensure_unique_name(dst_name)
    src = self.get_element(src_name)
    et = src.etype
    new_idx = self._parser.next_index(et)

    self._parser.clone_records(et, src.index, new_idx)

    # Update NUM
    current_count = self._parser.num_instances(et)
    self._parser.set_num_instances(et, current_count + 1)

    # Set new NAME (preserve descriptor in values[1:])
    name_rec = self._parser.get(et, new_idx, Common.NAME)
    if name_rec:
        name_rec.values[0] = dst_name
        name_rec.raw_line = ""  # mark dirty for re-serialisation

    # Clear connectivity on the copy
    for con_param in (
        Common.CON,
        Common.NOZI,
        Common.NOZO,
        Common.NOZL,
        Common.NOZ,
    ):
        rec = self._parser.get(et, new_idx, con_param)
        if rec is not None:
            rec.values = ["0"] * len(rec.values)
            rec.raw_line = ""

    self._build_collections()
    return self.get_element(dst_name)

def move_element(self, name: str, target_index: int) -> None:
    """Change the index of an element within its type group.

    This swaps the element to a new index, useful for reordering.
    If *target_index* is already in use, the occupant is swapped to
    the source index.

    Parameters
    ----------
    name:
        Element NAME tag.
    target_index:
        Desired new index (must be >= 1).
    """
    elem = self.get_element(name)
    et = elem.etype
    src_idx = elem.index

    if target_index < 1:
        raise ValueError("Target index must be >= 1")
    if target_index == src_idx:
        return

    # Check if target is occupied
    target_recs = self._parser.get_all(et, target_index)
    if target_recs:
        # Swap: move target to a temp index, move source to target, move temp to source
        temp_idx = self._parser.next_index(et) + 1000
        self.reindex_element(et, target_index, temp_idx)
        self.reindex_element(et, src_idx, target_index)
        self.reindex_element(et, temp_idx, src_idx)
    else:
        self.reindex_element(et, src_idx, target_index)

    self._build_collections()

connect_elements(
    name1_or_pairs: str | list[tuple[str, str] | tuple[str, str, str]],
    name2: str | None = None,
    pipe_name: str | None = None,
) -> None

Connect elements.

Can be called with two names::

model.connect_elements("L1", "P1")

Or with a list of pairs::

model.connect_elements([("L1", "P1"), ("L2", "P1")])

If neither element is a pipe, a new pipe is auto-created and connected to both elements. You may provide pipe_name (or a third item in each tuple for list mode) to name the auto-created pipe.

Source code in pykorf\model.py 
def connect_elements(
    self,
    name1_or_pairs: str | list[tuple[str, str] | tuple[str, str, str]],
    name2: str | None = None,
    pipe_name: str | None = None,
) -> None:
    """Connect elements.

    Can be called with two names::

        model.connect_elements("L1", "P1")

    Or with a list of pairs::

        model.connect_elements([("L1", "P1"), ("L2", "P1")])

    If neither element is a pipe, a new pipe is auto-created and
    connected to both elements. You may provide ``pipe_name`` (or a
    third item in each tuple for list mode) to name the auto-created pipe.
    """
    from pykorf.connectivity import connect

    if isinstance(name1_or_pairs, list):
        for pair in name1_or_pairs:
            if len(pair) == 2:
                n1, n2 = pair
                p_name = None
            elif len(pair) == 3:
                n1, n2, p_name = pair
            else:
                raise ValueError(
                    "Each connection pair must be (name1, name2) or "
                    "(name1, name2, pipe_name)."
                )
            self.connect_elements(n1, n2, pipe_name=p_name)
    else:
        if name2 is None:
            raise ValueError("name2 is required when name1 is a string")
        elem1 = self.get_element(name1_or_pairs)
        elem2 = self.get_element(name2)
        if elem1.etype == Element.PIPE or elem2.etype == Element.PIPE:
            if pipe_name is not None:
                raise ValueError(
                    "pipe_name can only be provided when connecting two "
                    "non-PIPE elements."
                )
            connect(self, name1_or_pairs, name2)
            return

        new_pipe_name = pipe_name or self._next_auto_pipe_name()
        self._add_element_internal(Element.PIPE, new_pipe_name)
        connect(self, new_pipe_name, name1_or_pairs)
        connect(self, new_pipe_name, name2)

docs.title: get_elements_by_type
docs.text:
get_elements_by_type(etype: str) -> list[BaseElement]

Return all instances of a given element type (index >= 1).

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
etype: KDF element type keyword, e.g. 'PIPE', 'PUMP', 'FO'.

Source code in pykorf\model.py 
def get_elements_by_type(self, etype: str) -> list[BaseElement]:
    """Return all instances of a given element type (index >= 1).

    Parameters
    ----------
    etype:
        KDF element type keyword, e.g. ``'PIPE'``, ``'PUMP'``, ``'FO'``.
    """
    collection = self._collection_for_etype(etype)
    if collection is None:
        return []
    return [elem for idx, elem in sorted(collection.items()) if idx >= 1]

docs.title: compact_indices
docs.text:
compact_indices(etype: str | None = None) -> None

Reorder element indices to be continuous (1..N) and update NUM.

If etype is None, compacts all element types.

Source code in pykorf\model.py 
def compact_indices(self, etype: str | None = None) -> None:
    """Reorder element indices to be continuous (1..N) and update NUM.

    If *etype* is None, compacts all element types.
    """
    etypes = [etype.upper()] if etype else Element.ALL
    for et in etypes:
        if et in (Element.GEN, Element.SYMBOL, Element.TOOLS, Element.PSEUDO):
            continue

        # Get current indices (sorted)
        indices = sorted(
            {
                r.index
                for r in self._parser.records
                if r.element_type == et and r.index and r.index >= 1
            }
        )
        if not indices:
            self._parser.set_num_instances(et, 0)
            continue

        # Map old -> new
        for i, old_idx in enumerate(indices, 1):
            if old_idx != i:
                self.reindex_element(et, old_idx, i)

        # Update NUM count
        self._parser.set_num_instances(et, len(indices))

    self._build_collections()

docs.title: Core Concepts
docs.text: Understanding the fundamental concepts of pyKorf.

docs.title: Architecture Overview
docs.text:
graph TD
    A[Model] --> B[Parser]
    A --> C[Elements]
    A --> D[Cases]
    A --> E[Connectivity]
    A --> F[Validation]

    B --> G[KDF File]
    C --> H[Pipe]
    C --> I[Pump]
    C --> J[Valve]
    C --> K[...]

    D --> L[Case 1]
    D --> M[Case 2]
    D --> N[Case 3]

docs.title: Model Persistence Contract
docs.text:
The most important concept in pyKorf is the persistence contract:

  1. Load: Model(path) reads the KDF file into memory
  2. Modify: All operations modify only the in-memory state
  3. Save: Changes are written only when you call model.save() or model.save_as()
model = Model("input.kdf")  # 1. Load
model.update_element(...)    # 2. Modify (in memory only)
model.save("output.kdf")     # 3. Save

Unsaved Changes

If you don't call save(), all changes are lost when the Python process ends.

docs.title: Element Indexing
docs.text:
Every element type in KORF has:

  • Index 0: Default template (defines default values)
  • Index 1+: Real instances
# Template (index 0)
template = model.pipes[0]  # Default values for new pipes

# Real instances (index 1+)
pipe1 = model.pipes[1]
pipe2 = model.pipes[2]

docs.title: KDF File Format
docs.text:
Understanding the KDF format helps when working with pyKorf:

Aspect Detail Encoding latin-1 (not UTF-8) Line Endings \r\n (Windows CRLF) Multi-case Semicolon-delimited: "50;55;20" Calculated Marked with ";C" suffix Format \ETYPE,index,PARAM,value,...

Example KDF record: 

"\PIPE",1,"TFLOW","50;55;20",20,"t/h"

This represents: - Element type: PIPE - Index: 1 - Parameter: TFLOW - Values: input="50;55;20", calculated=20, unit="t/h"

docs.title: Element Types
docs.text: KDF Keyword pyKorf Class Description \GEN General Global settings \PIPE Pipe Process line/pipe \PUMP Pump Centrifugal or PD pump \VALVE Valve Control valve \FEED Feed Source boundary \PROD Product Sink boundary \HX HeatExchanger Heat exchanger \COMP Compressor Compressor \VESSEL Vessel Pressure vessel \TEE Tee Tee piece \JUNC Junction Multi-pipe junction

docs.title: Constants and Definitions
docs.text:
Always use constants from pykorf.definitions instead of hardcoded strings:

# CORRECT
from pykorf.definitions import Element, Pipe, Common

model.add_element(Element.PIPE, "L1", {Pipe.LEN: 100})

# INCORRECT
model.add_element("PIPE", "L1", {"LEN": 100})

docs.title: Multi-Case Values
docs.text:
KORF supports running multiple scenarios (cases) in a single file:

# Single value applies to all cases
model.update_element("L1", {Pipe.TFLOW: "50"})

# Multi-case values (3 cases)
model.update_element("L1", {Pipe.TFLOW: "50;55;20"})

docs.title: Connectivity Model
docs.text:
In KORF, pipes are the "edges" and equipment are "nodes":

Feed → Pipe → Pump → Pipe → Product
  • Pipes don't have explicit connections; they're referenced by other elements
  • Equipment (Pump, Valve, etc.) have CON records: [inlet_pipe, outlet_pipe]
  • Boundaries (Feed, Product) have NOZL records referencing one pipe

docs.title: Fluent Interface
docs.text:
Many pyKorf APIs support method chaining:

from pykorf import Query, attr

results = (
    Query(model)
    .pipes
    .where(attr("diameter_inch") == "6")
    .where(attr("length_m") > 100)
    .order_by("name")
    .all()
)

docs.title: Context Managers
docs.text:
Use context managers for structured operations:

from pykorf.log import log_operation

with log_operation("process_model", path="model.kdf"):
    model = Model("model.kdf")
    model.validate()
    model.save()

docs.title: Type Safety
docs.text:
pyKorf provides Pydantic models for type-safe data handling:

from pykorf.types import PipeData, FlowParameters

pipe = PipeData(
    name="L1",
    diameter_inch="6",
    length_m=100.0,
    flow=FlowParameters(mass_flow_t_h=[50, 55, 20])
)

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: Get up and running with pyKorf in minutes.

docs.title: Loading an Existing Model
docs.text:
from pykorf import Model

# Load an existing KDF file
model = Model("Pumpcases.kdf")

# Display basic information
print(f"Version: {model.version}")
print(f"Cases: {model.general.case_descriptions}")
print(f"Pipes: {model.num_pipes}")
print(f"Pumps: {model.num_pumps}")

docs.title: Creating a New Model
docs.text:
from pykorf import Model
from pykorf.definitions import Element, Pipe, Feed, Prod

# Create a blank model from the default template
model = Model()

# Add elements
model.add_element(Element.FEED, "S1", {Feed.PRES: "100"})
model.add_element(Element.PROD, "D1", {Prod.PRES: "50"})

# Connect them with a pipe
model.connect_elements("S1", "D1", pipe_name="L1")

# Configure the pipe
model.update_element("L1", {
    Pipe.LEN: 100,
    Pipe.DIA: "6",
    Pipe.TFLOW: "50"
})

# Save the model
model.save("new_model.kdf")

docs.title: Accessing Elements
docs.text:
# By name (recommended)
pipe = model["L1"]
pump = model["P1"]

# By index
pipe = model.pipes[1]  # Index 1 is the first real pipe
pump = model.pumps[1]

# List all elements
for elem in model.elements:
    print(f"{elem.name}: {elem.etype}")

docs.title: Modifying Elements
docs.text:
# Update parameters
model.update_element("L1", {
    Pipe.LEN: 200,
    Pipe.MAT: "Steel"
})

# Update multi-case values
model.update_element("L1", {
    Pipe.TFLOW: "50;55;20"  # 3 cases
})

docs.title: Multi-Case Analysis
docs.text:
from pykorf import Model, CaseSet

model = Model("Pumpcases.kdf")
cases = CaseSet(model)

# List cases
print(f"Cases: {cases.names}")  # ['NORMAL', 'RATED', 'MINIMUM']

# Get flow table
flows = cases.pipe_flows_table()
for row in flows[:3]:
    print(row)

# Activate specific cases
cases.activate_cases([1, 2])  # Run only cases 1 and 2
model.save()

docs.title: Querying Elements
docs.text:
from pykorf import Model, Query, attr

model = Model("Pumpcases.kdf")
q = Query(model)

# Find all pipes
pipes = q.pipes.all()

# Filter by diameter
large_pipes = q.pipes.where(attr("diameter_inch") == "8").all()

# Find by name pattern
p_elements = q.by_name("P*").all()

# Complex queries
results = (
    q.elements
    .where(attr("etype") == "PIPE")
    .where(attr("name").startswith("L"))
    .order_by("name")
    .limit(10)
    .all()
)

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
# Validate the model
issues = model.validate()

if issues:
    print(f"Found {len(issues)} issues:")
    for issue in issues:
        print(f"  - {issue}")
else:
    print("Model is valid!")

# Check connectivity
conn_issues = model.check_connectivity()
if conn_issues:
    print("Connectivity issues:", conn_issues)

docs.title: Export
docs.text:
from pykorf.export import export_to_json, export_to_excel

# Export to JSON
export_to_json(model, "model.json")

# Export to Excel
export_to_excel(model, "model.xlsx")

docs.text: Managing connections between elements in your hydraulic model.

docs.title: Connectivity Model
docs.text:
In KORF, pipes are the edges and equipment are the nodes:

Feed → Pipe1 → Pump → Pipe2 → Valve → Pipe3 → Product
  • Pipes don't have explicit connections; they're referenced by equipment
  • Equipment has CON records: [inlet_pipe, outlet_pipe]
  • Boundaries (Feed/Product) have NOZL records: [pipe_index]

docs.title: Connect Two Elements
docs.text:
# Connect two elements (creates pipe if needed)
model.connect_elements("Feed1", "Pump1")

# Specify pipe name
model.connect_elements("Pump1", "Product1", pipe_name="Discharge")

docs.title: Connect Multiple Pairs
docs.text:
# Connect multiple pairs at once
model.connect_elements([
    ("Feed1", "Pump1"),
    ("Pump1", "Valve1"),
    ("Valve1", "Product1"),
])

# With custom pipe names
model.connect_elements([
    ("Feed1", "Pump1", "Suction"),
    ("Pump1", "Product1", "Discharge"),
])

docs.title: Connecting Pipes Directly
docs.text:
# If both elements are pipes, you need an intermediate element
# This will fail:
# model.connect_elements("L1", "L2")  # Error!

# Instead, use a Tee or Junction
model.add_element(Element.TEE, "T1")
model.connect_elements("L1", "T1")
model.connect_elements("L2", "T1")
model.connect_elements("L3", "T1")  # Third leg

docs.title: Disconnect Two Elements
docs.text:
# Remove connection between elements
model.disconnect_elements("Pump1", "Pipe1")

docs.title: Disconnect Multiple
docs.text:
model.disconnect_elements([
    ("Pump1", "Pipe1"),
    ("Valve1", "Pipe2"),
])

docs.title: Get Connections
docs.text:
# Get all elements connected to a pipe
connected = model.get_connection("L1")
print(connected)  # ['Pump1', 'Valve1']

# Get connections for equipment
# (returns connected pipe names)
connected = model.get_connection("Pump1")
print(connected)  # ['Suction', 'Discharge']

docs.title: Check Connectivity
docs.text:
# Validate all connections
issues = model.check_connectivity()
if issues:
    for issue in issues:
        print(issue)
else:
    print("All connections valid")

docs.title: Find Unconnected Elements
docs.text:
# Find elements with open connections
unconnected = model.get_unconnected_elements()
print(f"Unconnected: {unconnected}")

docs.title: Equipment with CON
docs.text:
# Pump, Valve, Compressor, etc.
# CON = [inlet_pipe, outlet_pipe]

pump = model["P1"]
rec = pump._get(Common.CON)
print(rec.values)  # [1, 2] - pipe indices

docs.title: Feed/Product with NOZL
docs.text:
# Feed and Product boundaries
# NOZL = [pipe_index]

feed = model["Source"]
rec = feed._get(Common.NOZL)  # or Common.NOZ for older versions
print(rec.values)  # [1] - single pipe index

docs.title: Tee with CON
docs.text:
# Tee has 6 values in CON:
# [C_in, C_out, M_in, M_out, B_in, B_out]
# (Combined, Main, Branch)

tee = model["T1"]
rec = tee._get(Common.CON)
print(rec.values)  # [1, 0, 2, 0, 3, 0]

docs.title: Junction with NOZI/NOZO
docs.text:
# Junction has multiple NOZI/NOZO records
# NOZI = inlet nozzles
# NOZO = outlet nozzles

junc = model["J1"]
for rec in junc.records():
    if rec.param in (Common.NOZI, Common.NOZO):
        print(f"{rec.param}: {rec.values}")

docs.title: Update Pipe References
docs.text:
When you delete or move a pipe, references are automatically updated:

# Delete a pipe
model.delete_element("L1")

# P1's CON record is automatically updated
# (L1's index is replaced with 0)

docs.title: Manual Reference Updates
docs.text:
from pykorf.connectivity import update_pipe_references

# Manually update references (rarely needed)
update_pipe_references(model, old_pipe_index=5, new_pipe_index=10)

docs.title: Text Visualization
docs.text:
# Get text representation of connectivity
viz = model.visualize()
print(viz)

# Output:
# === Model Layout ===
# [PIPE]
#   L1          idx=1   (1000, 2000)
# [PUMP]
#   P1          idx=1   (1500, 2000)
# [Connections]
#   L1 --> [P1] --> L2

docs.title: Network Graph
docs.text:
# Generate interactive HTML
model.visualize_network("network.html")

docs.title: 1. Connect in Logical Order
docs.text:
# Build the model in flow direction
model.connect_elements("Feed", "Pump")
model.connect_elements("Pump", "Valve")
model.connect_elements("Valve", "Product")

docs.title: 2. Name Pipes Meaningfully
docs.text:
# Good names
model.connect_elements("P1", "V1", pipe_name="P1_to_V1")
model.connect_elements("V1", "Prod", pipe_name="V1_Discharge")

docs.title: 3. Validate After Changes
docs.text:
# After modifying connectivity
model.connect_elements(...)
issues = model.check_connectivity()
if issues:
    raise ValueError(f"Connectivity issues: {issues}")

docs.title: 4. Check for Orphans
docs.text:
# Before saving
unconnected = model.get_unconnected_elements()
if unconnected:
    logger.warning(f"Unconnected elements: {unconnected}")

docs.title: Common Issues
docs.text:
# Issue: Cannot connect two pipes
try:
    model.connect_elements("L1", "L2")
except ConnectivityError as e:
    print("Use a Tee or Junction between pipes")

# Issue: Element already connected
try:
    model.connect_elements("Pump1", "L3")
except ConnectivityError as e:
    print("Pump1 may already have both connections filled")

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Validation
  • Working with Elements

docs.title: Export & Import
docs.text: Exporting and importing model data in various formats.

docs.title: Export Formats
docs.text:
pyKorf supports multiple export formats:

  • JSON - Machine-readable, hierarchical
  • YAML - Human-readable, hierarchical
  • Excel - Multi-sheet workbook
  • CSV - Tabular data files

docs.title: JSON Export
docs.text:
from pykorf.export import export_to_json
from pykorf.types import ExportOptions

# Basic export
export_to_json(model, "output.json")

# With options
options = ExportOptions(
    include_results=True,
    include_geometry=True,
    include_connectivity=True,
    indent=2
)
export_to_json(model, "output.json", options=options)

docs.title: JSON Structure
docs.text:
{
  "metadata": {
    "version": "KORF_3.6",
    "file": "model.kdf",
    "num_cases": 3
  },
  "elements": {
    "pipes": [...],
    "pumps": [...]
  },
  "connectivity": [...]
}

docs.title: YAML Export
docs.text:
from pykorf.export import export_to_yaml

export_to_yaml(model, "output.yaml")

docs.title: Excel Export
docs.text:
from pykorf.export import export_to_excel

export_to_excel(model, "output.xlsx", include_results=True)

docs.title: Excel Sheets
docs.text:
• Summary - Model overview
  • Pipes - Pipe data
  • Pumps - Pump data
  • Cases - Case definitions

docs.title: CSV Export
docs.text:
from pykorf.export import export_to_csv

# Export all elements
export_to_csv(model, "./csv_export/")

# Export specific type
export_to_csv(
    model,
    "./csv_export/",
    element_type="pipes",  # or "pumps", "valves"
    include_results=True
)

docs.title: Export Options
docs.text: Option Default Description include_results True Include calculated values include_geometry True Include XY positions include_connectivity True Include connection data indent 2 JSON indentation encoding "utf-8" Output encoding

docs.title: Import
docs.text:
Currently, pyKorf only supports importing KDF files directly:

# Load KDF
model = Model("input.kdf")

# Modify
model.update_element(...)

# Save
model.save("output.kdf")

docs.title: From JSON (Manual)
docs.text:
import json
from pykorf import Model
from pykorf.definitions import Element, Pipe

# Load JSON data
with open("data.json") as f:
    data = json.load(f)

# Create model
model = Model()

# Add elements from JSON
for pipe_data in data["elements"]["pipes"]:
    model.add_element(Element.PIPE, pipe_data["name"], {
        Pipe.LEN: pipe_data["length_m"],
        Pipe.DIA: pipe_data["diameter_inch"]
    })

model.save()

docs.title: 1. Export for Backup
docs.text:
# Before making changes
export_to_json(model, "backup.json")

# Make changes
model.update_element(...)

# Save
model.save()

docs.title: 2. Export for Analysis
docs.text:
# Export to Excel for analysis in pandas
export_to_excel(model, "analysis.xlsx")

# Or use Results directly
from pykorf import Results
results = Results(model)
df = results.to_dataframe()

docs.title: 3. Selective Export
docs.text:
# Export only what you need
options = ExportOptions(
    include_results=False,  # Smaller file
    include_geometry=False  # No positions
)
export_to_json(model, "minimal.json", options=options)

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text: • Working with Elements

docs.title: Loading and Saving Models
docs.text: Working with KDF files in pyKorf.

docs.title: From File
docs.text:
from pykorf import Model

# Load an existing KDF file
model = Model("path/to/model.kdf")

docs.title: Creating Blank Models
docs.text:
# Create a blank model from the default template
model = Model()  # Uses pykorf/library/New.kdf

# The template includes default values for all element types

docs.title: From Template
docs.text:
from pathlib import Path

# Load from a custom template
template_path = Path("my_template.kdf")
model = Model(template_path)

docs.title: Basic Properties
docs.text:
# File information
print(model.path)        # Path to the loaded file
print(model.version)     # KDF version (e.g., "KORF_3.6")

# Element counts
print(model.num_pipes)
print(model.num_pumps)
print(model.num_cases)

# Summary
summary = model.summary()
print(summary)
# {
#     'file': 'model.kdf',
#     'version': 'KORF_3.6',
#     'cases': ['NORMAL', 'RATED', 'MINIMUM'],
#     'num_pipes': 10,
#     'num_pumps': 2,
#     ...
# }

docs.title: General Settings
docs.text:
# Access general settings
print(model.general.case_descriptions)  # ['NORMAL', 'RATED', 'MINIMUM']
print(model.general.units)              # 'Metric'
print(model.general.project_name)       # Project name

docs.title: Save Over Original
docs.text:
# Overwrite the original file
model.save()

docs.title: Save As New File
docs.text:
# Save to a new file
model.save("modified_model.kdf")

# Or use save_as (alias)
model.save_as("backup.kdf")

docs.title: Save With Validation
docs.text:
# Validate before saving
issues = model.validate()
if issues:
    print("Validation issues found:")
    for issue in issues:
        print(f"  - {issue}")
    # Decide whether to save anyway
else:
    model.save()

docs.title: Working with Paths
docs.text:
from pathlib import Path

# pyKorf uses pathlib.Path internally
model = Model(Path("models") / "pump.kdf")

# Get absolute path
abs_path = model.path.resolve()

# Check if file exists
if model.path.exists():
    print("File exists")

docs.title: Common Errors
docs.text:
from pykorf import Model
from pykorf.exceptions import ParseError, KorfError

try:
    model = Model("corrupted.kdf")
except ParseError as e:
    print(f"Failed to parse: {e}")
    # e.context may contain file_path, line_number
except FileNotFoundError:
    print("File not found")
except KorfError as e:
    print(f"Korf error: {e}")

docs.title: Validation on Load
docs.text:
model = Model("model.kdf")

# Always validate after loading if file integrity is critical
issues = model.validate()
if issues:
    raise ValueError(f"Model has {len(issues)} validation issues")

docs.title: 1. Always Save Explicitly
docs.text:
# Changes are in-memory only until save()
model.update_element("L1", {Pipe.LEN: 200})
# ... more changes ...
model.save()  # Don't forget this!

docs.title: 2. Backup Before Modifying
docs.text:
from shutil import copy

# Backup original
copy("model.kdf", "model_backup.kdf")

# Now modify
model = Model("model.kdf")
# ... changes ...
model.save()

docs.title: 3. Use Context Managers for Batch Operations
docs.text:
from pykorf.log import log_operation

with log_operation("batch_update", file="model.kdf"):
    model = Model("model.kdf")

    # Multiple changes
    for i in range(1, model.num_pipes + 1):
        pipe = model.pipes[i]
        model.update_element(pipe.name, {Pipe.LEN: 100})

    model.save()

docs.title: 4. Handle Encoding Properly
docs.text:
pyKorf handles latin-1 encoding automatically. Don't try to convert:

# Don't do this
with open("model.kdf", "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:  # Wrong!
    ...

# pyKorf handles encoding internally
model = Model("model.kdf")  # Correct

docs.title: Multi-Case Analysis
docs.text: Working with multiple simulation scenarios in KORF.

docs.title: Understanding Cases
docs.text:
KORF supports running multiple simulation scenarios (cases) in a single file. Each case can have different:

  • Flow rates
  • Pressures
  • Temperatures
  • Fluid properties

docs.title: Case Structure
docs.text:
from pykorf import Model, CaseSet

model = Model("Pumpcases.kdf")
cases = CaseSet(model)

# Case information
print(cases.names)      # ['NORMAL', 'RATED', 'MINIMUM']
print(cases.count)      # 3
print(cases.numbers)    # ['1', '2', '3']

docs.title: Setting Flows
docs.text:
# Set pipe flow for all cases
model.pipes[1].set_flow([50, 55, 20])

# Or as semicolon-delimited string
model.update_element("L1", {Pipe.TFLOW: "50;55;20"})

docs.title: Setting Pressures
docs.text:
# Set feed pressure for all cases
model.feeds[1].set_pressure([100, 120, 80])

# Set product back-pressure
model.products[1].set_pressure([50, 60, 40])

docs.title: Setting Temperatures
docs.text:
# Set pipe temperature (per case)
model.update_element("L1", {
    Pipe.TEMP: "25;30;20"  # 3 cases
})

docs.title: Activating Cases
docs.text:
Control which cases are included in the KORF report:

# Activate all cases
cases.activate_cases([1, 2, 3])

# Activate only specific cases
cases.activate_cases([1, 3])  # Run only cases 1 and 3

# Activate single case
cases.activate_cases([2])  # Run only case 2

docs.title: Getting Case Values
docs.text:
# Get value for specific case
value = cases.get_case_value("50;55;20", case_index=2)
print(value)  # "55"

# Single value applies to all cases
value = cases.get_case_value("100", case_index=3)
print(value)  # "100"

docs.title: Setting Case Values
docs.text:
# Update value for specific case
new_string = cases.set_case_value("50;55;20", case_index=2, new_value=60)
print(new_string)  # "50;60;20"

docs.title: Pipe Flow Table
docs.text:
# Get table of pipe flows
flows = cases.pipe_flows_table()

for row in flows:
    print(row)
# {
#     'pipe': 'L1',
#     'index': 1,
#     'NORMAL': '50',
#     'RATED': '55',
#     'MINIMUM': '20'
# }

docs.title: Custom Tables
docs.text:
# Create custom analysis table
table = []
for idx, pipe in model.pipes.items():
    if idx == 0:
        continue
    row = {
        'name': pipe.name,
        'diameter': pipe.diameter_inch,
    }
    # Add case values
    for i, case_name in enumerate(cases.names, 1):
        flow = cases.get_case_value(pipe.flow_string, i)
        row[case_name] = flow
    table.append(row)

docs.title: CaseSet Methods
docs.text:
cases = CaseSet(model)

# Validate case index
cases._validate_case(1)  # OK
cases._validate_case(5)  # Raises CaseError

# Set bulk values
cases.set_pipe_flows(1, [50, 55, 20])
cases.set_feed_pressures(1, [100, 110, 90])
cases.set_product_pressures(1, [50, 55, 45])

docs.title: 1. Always Use Lists for Multi-Case
docs.text:
# Good - clear intent
pipe.set_flow([50, 55, 20])

# OK but less clear
pipe.set_flow("50;55;20")

docs.title: 2. Match List Length to Case Count
docs.text:
# Ensure your values match the number of cases
if len(flows) != cases.count:
    raise ValueError(f"Expected {cases.count} values, got {len(flows)}")

pipe.set_flow(flows)

docs.title: 3. Document Case Meanings
docs.text:
# Add comments explaining cases
CASE_DEFINITIONS = {
    1: "NORMAL - Standard operating conditions",
    2: "RATED - Maximum design capacity",
    3: "MINIMUM - Minimum operating flow"
}

docs.title: 4. Use Descriptive Case Names
docs.text:
# Set meaningful case descriptions
model.general.case_descriptions = [
    "Summer Peak",
    "Winter Normal",
    "Startup",
    "Shutdown"
]

docs.title: 5. Validate Before Running
docs.text:
# Check all required parameters are set for all cases
for case_idx in range(1, cases.count + 1):
    for pipe in model.pipes.values():
        if pipe.index == 0:
            continue
        flow = cases.get_case_value(pipe.flow_string, case_idx)
        if not flow or flow == "":
            logger.warning(f"Pipe {pipe.name} has no flow for case {case_idx}")

docs.title: Example: Complete Case Setup
docs.text:
from pykorf import Model, CaseSet
from pykorf.definitions import Element, Pipe, Feed, Prod

# Create model
model = Model()
cases = CaseSet(model)

# Define cases
case_names = ["NORMAL", "RATED", "MINIMUM"]
model.general.case_descriptions = case_names

# Add elements
model.add_element(Element.FEED, "Source", {Feed.PRES: "100;110;90"})
model.add_element(Element.PROD, "Sink", {Prod.PRES: "50"})
model.connect_elements("Source", "Sink", pipe_name="MainLine")

# Set flows for all cases
model.update_element("MainLine", {Pipe.TFLOW: "100;120;80"})

# Verify
print("Case setup:")
print(f"  Names: {cases.names}")
print(f"  Count: {cases.count}")

flows = cases.pipe_flows_table()
for row in flows:
    print(f"  {row['name']}: {row['NORMAL']} / {row['RATED']} / {row['MINIMUM']}")

# Save
model.save("multi_case_model.kdf")

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Export & Import
  • Working with Elements

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: Ensuring model integrity with pyKorf's validation system.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
pyKorf provides comprehensive validation to ensure your KDF files are:

  • Structurally valid - Correct format and encoding
  • Semantically valid - Meaningful data and relationships
  • Complete - All required fields present

docs.title: Basic Validation
docs.text:
# Validate the entire model
issues = model.validate()

if issues:
    print(f"Found {len(issues)} issues:")
    for issue in issues:
        print(f"  - {issue}")
else:
    print("Model is valid!")

docs.title: Connectivity Validation
docs.text:
# Check element connections
issues = model.check_connectivity()

for issue in issues:
    print(issue)
# "Pump P1: inlet references pipe index 99 which does not exist"

docs.title: Layout Validation
docs.text:
# Check for layout issues
issues = model.check_layout()

for issue in issues:
    print(issue)
# "Element Pump1 at (1000.0, 2000.0) is outside layout bounds"

docs.title: 1. File Format Validation
docs.text:
• Valid encoding (latin-1)
  • Correct line endings
  • Valid CSV structure
  • Supported version

docs.title: 2. Structure Validation
docs.text:
• Required parameters present
  • Valid element indices
  • NUM records match actual counts
  • Element order correct

docs.title: 3. Content Validation
docs.text:
• Valid parameter values
  • Consistent units
  • Logical relationships
  • Complete connectivity

docs.title: 4. Semantic Validation
docs.text:
• Positive lengths
  • Valid diameters
  • Consistent pressures
  • Realistic temperatures

docs.title: Strict Mode
docs.text:
from pykorf.config import get_config

config = get_config()
config.validation.strict_mode = True

# Now validation will be stricter
issues = model.validate()

docs.title: Environment Variable
docs.text: export PYKORF_STRICT_VALIDATION=1

docs.title: Common Issues
docs.text: Issue Cause Solution Missing NAME record Element has no name Assign a name Invalid pipe reference CON points to non-existent pipe Fix connection NUM mismatch NUM record doesn't match count Regenerate indices Outside layout bounds XY coordinates invalid Reposition element Overlapping elements Two elements at same position Move one element

docs.title: Handling Validation Errors
docs.text:
from pykorf.exceptions import ValidationError

try:
    issues = model.validate()
    if issues:
        raise ValidationError(issues)
except ValidationError as e:
    print(f"Validation failed:")
    for issue in e.issues:
        print(f"  - {issue}")

docs.title: 1. Validate After Loading
docs.text:
model = Model("input.kdf")
issues = model.validate()
if issues:
    logger.warning(f"Loaded model has {len(issues)} issues")

docs.title: 2. Validate Before Saving
docs.text:
# Before saving critical models
if config.validation.check_connectivity_on_save:
    issues = model.check_connectivity()
    if issues:
        raise ValueError(f"Cannot save: {issues}")

model.save()

docs.title: 3. Log Validation Issues
docs.text:
from pykorf.log import get_logger

logger = get_logger()

issues = model.validate()
for issue in issues:
    if "ERROR" in issue:
        logger.error(issue)
    else:
        logger.warning(issue)

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Working with Elements
  • Export & Import

docs.title: Working with Elements
docs.text: Comprehensive guide to manipulating KORF elements.

docs.title: By Name (Recommended)
docs.text:
# Access elements by their NAME tag
pipe = model["L1"]
pump = model["P1"]
valve = model["CV1"]

docs.title: By Index
docs.text:
# Access by element index
# Index 0 is the template, 1+ are real instances
pipe = model.pipes[1]      # First real pipe
pump = model.pumps[1]      # First real pump

docs.title: Checking Existence
docs.text:
# Check if element exists
if "L1" in model:
    print("L1 exists")

# Or use try/except
try:
    elem = model["NONEXISTENT"]
except ElementNotFound:
    print("Not found")

docs.title: Common Properties
docs.text:
All elements have these properties:

elem = model["L1"]

print(elem.index)        # Element index (1, 2, 3, ...)
print(elem.etype)        # Element type ("PIPE", "PUMP", etc.)
print(elem.name)         # Element name tag ("L1")
print(elem.description)  # Description from NAME record
print(elem.notes)        # User notes

docs.title: Pipes
docs.text:
pipe = model["L1"]

# Geometry
print(pipe.diameter_inch)      # Nominal diameter
print(pipe.schedule)           # Pipe schedule
print(pipe.length_m)           # Length in meters
print(pipe.material)           # Material ("Steel", etc.)

# Flow
print(pipe.flow_string)        # Raw flow string (e.g., "50;55;20")
print(pipe.get_flow())         # List of flow values
print(pipe.flow_unit)          # Flow unit ("t/h")

# Results (after KORF calculation)
print(pipe.velocity)           # Velocities per case
print(pipe.pressure)           # Pressures per case
print(pipe.reynolds_number)    # Reynolds number
print(pipe.pressure_drop_per_100m)  # dP/100m

docs.title: Pumps
docs.text:
pump = model["P1"]

print(pump.pump_type)          # "Centrifugal" or "Reciprocating"
print(pump.efficiency)         # Pump efficiency
print(pump.power_kW)           # Calculated power
print(pump.head_m)             # Calculated head
print(pump.flow_m3h)           # Calculated flow

docs.title: Update Parameters
docs.text:
from pykorf.definitions import Pipe, Pump

# Update single parameter
model.update_element("L1", {Pipe.LEN: 200})

# Update multiple parameters
model.update_element("L1", {
    Pipe.LEN: 200,
    Pipe.MAT: "Stainless Steel",
    Pipe.SCH: "40"
})

# Update multi-case values
model.update_element("L1", {
    Pipe.TFLOW: "50;55;20"  # 3 cases
})

docs.title: Setting Position
docs.text:
from pykorf.layout import set_position

# Set element position
set_position(model, "L1", 1000.0, 2000.0)

# Or on the element directly
from pykorf.layout import set_position as set_pos
pipe = model["L1"]
set_pos(pipe, 1000.0, 2000.0)

docs.title: Add Equipment
docs.text:
from pykorf.definitions import Element, Pump, Valve

# Add a pump
model.add_element(Element.PUMP, "P2", {
    Pump.TYPE: "Centrifugal"
})

# Add a valve
model.add_element(Element.VALVE, "CV2", {
    Valve.TYPE: "Linear",
    Valve.CV: "50"
})

docs.title: Add Pipes with Connectivity
docs.text:
# Pipes must be created via connect_elements
model.connect_elements("P1", "V1", pipe_name="L10")

docs.title: Batch Add
docs.text:
from pykorf.definitions import Element, Feed, Prod

# Add multiple elements at once
elements = model.add_elements([
    (Element.FEED, "S2", {Feed.PRES: "100"}),
    (Element.FEED, "S3", {Feed.PRES: "120"}),
    (Element.PROD, "D2", {Prod.PRES: "50"}),
])

docs.title: Copy Single Element
docs.text:
# Copy element with new name
copy = model.copy_element("L1", "L1_copy")

# Connectivity is cleared on the copy
print(copy.name)  # "L1_copy"

docs.title: Copy Multiple
docs.text:
# Copy multiple elements
copies = model.copy_elements([
    ("L1", "L1_copy"),
    ("P1", "P1_copy"),
])

docs.title: Delete Single
docs.text:
# Delete by name
model.delete_element("L10")

docs.title: Delete Multiple
docs.text:
# Delete multiple elements
model.delete_elements(["L10", "L11", "L12"])

docs.title: Delete with Connectivity Update
docs.text:
When you delete a pipe, pyKorf automatically updates references:

# If L1 is connected to P1 and V1
model.delete_element("L1")

# P1 and V1 will have their CON records updated
# (pipe index set to 0)

docs.title: Reorder Indices
docs.text:
# Move element to different index
model.move_element("L5", target_index=10)

docs.title: Compact Indices
docs.text:
# Reorder all indices to be continuous (1, 2, 3, ...)
model.compact_indices()  # All element types
model.compact_indices("PIPE")  # Just pipes

docs.title: Iterate Elements
docs.text:
# All real elements (index >= 1)
for elem in model.elements:
    print(f"{elem.name}: {elem.etype}")

# Specific type
for idx, pipe in model.pipes.items():
    if idx == 0:
        continue  # Skip template
    print(f"Pipe {idx}: {pipe.name}")

docs.title: Filter by Type
docs.text:
# Get all elements of a type
pipes = model.get_elements_by_type("PIPE")
pumps = model.get_elements_by_type("PUMP")

docs.title: 1. Use Constants
docs.text:
# Always use constants from definitions
from pykorf.definitions import Element, Pipe, Common

model.update_element("L1", {Pipe.LEN: 100})  # Good
model.update_element("L1", {"LEN": 100})     # Bad

docs.title: 2. Handle Missing Elements
docs.text:
try:
    elem = model[name]
except ElementNotFound:
    logger.warning(f"Element {name} not found")
    return

docs.title: 3. Validate After Bulk Changes
docs.text:
# After many changes
model.update_elements({...})
issues = model.validate()
if issues:
    logger.warning(f"Validation issues: {issues}")

docs.title: 4. Use Descriptive Names
docs.text:
# Good names
cooling_water_feed
main_process_line
product_transfer_pump

# Avoid single letters
L1  # OK if following existing convention
P1  # OK if following existing convention
